title: Quiz: T2 Measurement
description: Test your understanding of dephasing measurements, Ramsey, echo, and CPMG sequences

questions.question: What is the key difference between T2* and T2 measurements?

options.text: T2* uses longer delay times than T2
T2 uses a refocusing pulse to cancel slow noise, T2* does not
T2* measures energy relaxation, T2 measures dephasing
T2 requires cryogenic temperatures, T2* does not

questions.explanation: T2 (Hahn echo) uses a refocusing pi pulse in the middle of the sequence that reverses the phase accumulation from slow (quasi-static) noise. T2* (Ramsey) has no refocusing pulse, so it measures dephasing from all noise sources including slow frequency fluctuations. This is why T2* <= T2.

questions.question: What is the correct Ramsey pulse sequence?

options.text: X - wait - X - measure
X/2 - wait - X/2 - measure
X/2 - wait - X - wait - X/2 - measure
X - wait - X/2 - measure

questions.explanation: The Ramsey sequence is: X/2 (pi/2 pulse) to create a superposition, free precession during the wait time, then a second X/2 pulse to convert phase into population for measurement. The X/2 - wait - X - wait - X/2 describes the spin echo sequence.

questions.question: For a qubit with T1 = 100 us and T2 = 150 us, what can you conclude?

options.text: The measurement is likely incorrect since T2 > 2*T1
The qubit has exceptional coherence properties
Pure dephasing dominates over relaxation
The echo sequence was not performed correctly

questions.explanation: The fundamental limit is T2 <= 2*T1. With T1 = 100 us, the maximum possible T2 is 200 us. A measured T2 = 150 us is physically possible, but T2 > 2*T1 would indicate measurement error. The value 150 us suggests the qubit is nearly T1-limited with minimal pure dephasing.

questions.question: What does the frequency of Ramsey fringes tell you?

options.text: The T2* dephasing time
The detuning between drive frequency and qubit frequency
The T1 relaxation time
The coupling strength to the resonator

questions.explanation: The Ramsey fringe frequency equals the detuning between the qubit frequency and the drive/reference frequency: f_fringe = |f_qubit - f_drive|. This makes Ramsey a powerful tool for precise frequency calibration.

questions.question: How does CPMG differ from a single Hahn echo?

options.text: CPMG uses X pulses instead of Y pulses
CPMG applies multiple refocusing pulses, filtering higher frequency noise
CPMG measures T1 while Hahn echo measures T2
CPMG requires no initial pi/2 pulse

questions.explanation: CPMG (Carr-Purcell-Meiboom-Gill) uses multiple refocusing pulses instead of just one. This creates a higher-frequency filter function, suppressing noise at frequencies up to N/(2*tau_total) where N is the number of pulses. More pulses generally give longer effective T2.

questions.question: What does it mean if T2* shows Gaussian decay rather than exponential decay?

options.text: The qubit has very long coherence times
Low-frequency (1/f) noise dominates the dephasing
The measurement has high statistical uncertainty
T1 relaxation is the dominant decay mechanism

questions.explanation: Gaussian decay in Ramsey (T2*) experiments indicates that quasi-static, low-frequency noise (often 1/f noise) dominates the dephasing. This noise is well-described by a Gaussian distribution of frequency fluctuations, leading to Gaussian decay of the coherence envelope.

questions.question: Dynamical decoupling sequences can extend T2 beyond the 2*T1 limit.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. The limit T2 <= 2*T1 is fundamental and arises because T1 processes (spontaneous emission) also cause dephasing. Dynamical decoupling can only suppress pure dephasing; it cannot remove the contribution from T1. The best achievable T2 with perfect dynamical decoupling is exactly 2*T1.

questions.question: If a qubit has T1 = 80 us, T2* = 20 us, and T2_echo = 70 us, what is the pure dephasing time T_phi from the echo measurement?

options.text: T_phi = 56 us
T_phi = 140 us
T_phi = 280 us
T_phi = 35 us
